g.data-narration: NFA extends DFA by allowing multiple transitions for the same input symbol, enabling more flexible pattern matching.
g.text: can lead to SETS of states, not
• Easier to construct for complex patterns
• Used in lexical analyzers & pattern matching

g.data-narration: An NFA is formally defined as a 5-tuple M = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) - learn each component to understand the structure.
g.text: NFA Formal Definition: M = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F)

g.data-narration: The key difference lies in the transition function: DFA returns one state, NFA returns a SET of possible states.
A string is accepted by NFA if AT LEAST ONE computational path leads to a final state - existence, not universality!
ε-transitions allow moving between states without consuming input, crucial for modeling optional patterns.
ε-closure of a state includes itself and all states reachable via zero or more ε-transitions.
g.text: ε*(q) = { all states reachable via 0+ ε-transitions }

g.data-narration: Let's trace string '001' through our NFA that accepts 0ⁿ1 (n≥1). Watch how multiple paths are explored.
This NFA accepts strings 'ab' optionally followed by 'c'. Notice the self-loop on final state for 'c'.
Subset construction converts NFA to DFA. Each DFA state represents a SET of NFA states reachable simultaneously.
g.text: DFA State = Set of NFA states

g.data-narration: Let's convert an NFA with two states. The start DFA state is ε*(q₀), then we compute all possible transitions.
The algorithm: compute ε-closure of start state, then for each input symbol apply δ and ε-closure to get new DFA states.
The equivalence theorem states every NFA has an equivalent DFA - they recognize exactly the same class: Regular Languages.
g.text: Every NFA has an equivalent DFA

g.data-narration: Remember these pitfalls: NFA accepts if ANY path accepts, compute ε-closure properly, and empty set ∅ is a valid DFA state.
In summary: NFA uses set-based transitions, accepts via existence of path, has ε-transitions, and is equivalent to DFA.
